evidence.quote: And the minute you sever that connection, so the minute someone's like, "Ah, I don't want to maintain this dumb OpenClaw," is the minute the agent is not really that useful anymore.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And that's why I think it has started to shift to a more one agent per company model because for now, the ideal is you basically set up a forward deployed engineer or someone with that profile who's responsible for making sure that that agent is working for the whole company and then maybe you have some little team agents.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: But the model that I see working for us and for a lot of other companies, including the model companies, the model companies themselves are starting to see this, is when it comes to the like async agents, it's really a, you have one agent at the top that's like doing, sometimes it's everything.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: A lot of times it's a particular job that you've decided that everyone in the company needs an agent for, like data requests.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: That is so interesting, that point about you need to garden your agent because there's context you have to keep adding to it.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: So the idea, so what you're predicting here is companies will have this super agent that everyone can talk to.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: So basically what happened was Anthropic realized at some point that, with Claude Code, if you put an agent on your computer and it runs on your computer, it has access to everything that you have access to.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So they figured that out and then I think they figured out, along with their community, that once you have a coding agent on your computer that can build anything, it's actually really good for any kind of work you want to do.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And then I think OpenAI made a couple of different bets, but their main bet on a programming agent was the earlier versions of Codex were very technical and they were super smart, but they were a little bit autistic.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: I'm never like this, and that's because I literally just have Codex gather all my emails with Quora, which is our email agent.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And I think the reverse is actually starting to happen and be really, really valuable in a way that I did not expect, which is take the AI agent that you use all the time on your computer and put a browser in it so it can see everything you're doing.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: Okay, so yeah, I'm using Proof or really any website, maybe PostHog or whatever, and I'm doing it inside of my agent and the agent has access to the website.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: When I run the agent on that website, I'm using my tokens.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And so, it puts SaaS back in this place where yeah, you want to make it friendly for an agent and everyone's got a CLI now, you want to make the HTML really usable.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: But once you do that, you actually don't really need to think about having an AI surface that's primarily going to be the thing that users use in the sense that you don't need to build an agent necessarily into your product.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And now people are doing CLI so it's meant for an agent to use independently of a human.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And we're moving into this new paradigm, I think, where the human and the agent are on the same piece of work together, and they're both doing things, and I need to have visibility into what the agent is doing, the agent has to have visibility into what I'm doing.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: I don't have to have a lot of the Word document formatting, or page breaks, or making tables or whatever, because the agent just does it.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: Their agent sends a bug report, and an agent bug report is way better than a human bug report.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And then we just get that, it becomes a GitHub issue, and then we can just send off an agent to fix it.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And you can't do that with everything but it's so much better, and you can see the glimmers of this very fast closed loop between I ran into something, a paper cut, a little feature I want, a little bug, and my agent just goes off and talks to the company agent and then the company agent just goes and fixes it.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: One is this super agent within a company that you chat with through Slack most likely that can go off and do work and answer questions, and then there's on your computer running Codex or Claude Code.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: I'm definitely not an agent maximalist, I really think we're going to have a lot of different agents that we use.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: When I have Codex interact with another agent, it can give so much more context about me and what I want than I would be able to type, and it can go back and forth talking about things that would take a long time for me to express directly to an agent that you get this speed up effect when you assume that your users are using Codex, or Claude Code, or Cowork as their basic way they access your app.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: We actually had to pause it because we started taking people off the waitlist, and OpenClaw is just a very a very hard agent harness to make work.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: But one of the things that we learned in that process is let's say you're building an agent product or any new software experience, what you would assume, let's say to set up an agent, is you need to build a little web interface or a little Slack workflow that asks people about, "Okay, who are you and what are you going to use this for?
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: If instead you just make a hard line of we are only going to service users who use Codex or Cowork, what happens is you just paste a prompt into Codex or Cowork, it goes and talks to the app, and the app can be either just a regular server or it can be its own agent, and Codex has so much information about you that it can just give it.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And also for a technical product like an agent, when something goes wrong I can just tell Codex, "Go fix it."
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And so, I think the whole paradigm starts to change when you assume that everyone's got an agent and those agents are talking to other agents in this really magical and important way.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: Well, I think it actually may save their margins, because right now all these companies are rushing to add a agent to their offering and thinking, "Oh, the agent is going to be the main way that people interact with me."
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So look for, I guess, a tool that could become the AI agent within your company.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: My slight tweak is when you're thinking about building your software for agents, the current model is I'm building a CLI that an agent uses, but they're using it in a sort of like they're being...
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: I delegated to task the agent and the agent's using the CLI.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And where I think it's going is you and the agent are using the app together.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: The agent's probably using the CLI, but you're using the web interface and they both need to be in sync.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And the thing that is becoming really clear is the whole Ford deployed engineer concept I think is for real and it comes out of every agent needs a human.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: We have an agent internally called Claudy, which runs our whole consulting practice and he spends a lot of time in Slack.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And so the data science team doesn't have to answer all the bullshit questions because there's another team building an agent that is set up to do that really well.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: You're dealing with the deeper questions that are harder for the team who's dealing with all the basic requests and building an agent to do that.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And I think the same is true when we did our quarterly planning for every at the end of 2025, we did it all with Notion Agents and we just had a bunch of Notion agents and we had really one Notion agent and then we had a top level company strategy and then we had everybody in the company just talked to an agent and it asked them about what happened last year, how did it go, what were your goals?
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: We publish all these guides and the guides are often agent, they're agent assisted and the agent is a co-author and they're intended to be read both by humans and by agents.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: In order to really apply it, the best way to do that is just have your agent ingest it and remember the next time I'm doing pricing to remind me of this guide and we'll go through it together or whatever.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And so you talk to the human about the story and the stuff that matters and the core ideas and the agent has all the details that it can then apply for you when you need it.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And if your company doesn't let you do it on your own time, I would try out some of these agent products like OpenClaw or Hermes or for less technical people, there's like Victor, we have OnePlus ones.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

entities.name: 前向部署工程师 (forward deployed engineer)

evidence.quote: And that's why I think it has started to shift to a more one agent per company model because for now, the ideal is you basically set up a forward deployed engineer or someone with that profile who's responsible for making sure that that agent is working for the whole company and then maybe you have some little team agents.

entities.name: 高级工程师基准 (senior engineer benchmark)
entities.how_described: 本集嘉宾自创的基准测试:让 AI 从第一性原理重写一个糟糕的代码库。结果 GPT 5.5 拿到 62 分,而真正的人类高级工程师能拿 80-90 分,证明 AI 目前缺乏重构判断力。

evidence.quote: So I made this senior, it's called the senior engineer benchmark, and it's like how good is AI versus a human engineer?
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So it could not do the senior engineer benchmark last time, and I turned the rock over again and now it's at a 60 out of 100, which is really good.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

entities.name: 自动化 (automation)
entities.how_described: 本集提出'自动化是个谎言'的观点:每次自动化一项任务,为了确保它不出错,就必须派一个真人去监督('保姆式'工作)。

evidence.quote: Automation is a lot.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: We have so much automation, so much AI, and I also work way more.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: Automation is a lie, in the sense that every time you automate something, in order to make sure the automation is working well you need a human on top of it making sure that it's working well.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: I've been feeling that we have so much automation, so much AI, and I also work way more.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And I think it's why even though the models are getting better at automation, I still hire engineers.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And it's another one of those places where you're like, hmm, automation was supposed to take away jobs, but it looks like it just created one or many.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1
